content.text: The dot product (inner product) of two vectors is the sum of the products of corresponding elements. It is the fastest similarity computation available — a single BLAS operation — and becomes equivalent to cosine similarity when vectors are unit-normalised. Understanding when and why to use it directly, rather than going through the cosine formula, is essential for building efficient embedding search systems.
Definition

attrs.latex: \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i b_i = a_1 b_1 + a_2 b_2 + \cdots + a_n b_n

content.text: Unlike cosine similarity, the dot product is not bounded to [−1, 1]. Its value depends on both the angle between the vectors and their magnitudes. A large magnitude vector paired with a small magnitude vector produces a moderate dot product even if they point in the same direction.

import numpy as np

a = np.array([3.0, 4.0])   # ‖a‖ = 5
b = np.array([0.6, 0.8])   # ‖b‖ = 1 (unit vector, same direction as a)
c = np.array([-3.0, 4.0]) # ‖c‖ = 5, different direction

print(np.dot(a, b))  # 5.0  — high: same direction, one is large
print(np.dot(a, c))  # 7.0  — even higher! c has large magnitude, partially aligned
print(np.dot(b, b))  # 1.0  — unit vector dot itself = 1 = cosine(0)

# Cosine similarity is NOT affected by magnitude
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
print(cosine_similarity([a], [b])[0, 0])  # 1.0 — identical direction
print(cosine_similarity([a], [c])[0, 0])  # 0.28 — different direction
When Dot Product == Cosine Similarity
When both vectors are unit-normalised (‖a‖ = ‖b‖ = 1):
This is the key reason embedding models apply
normalize_embeddings=True
— it enables using the cheaper dot product at search time while preserving cosine semantics. The normalisation cost is paid once at index time.

import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")

texts = ["cats are small carnivores", "dogs are loyal companions", "Python is a programming language"]

# Without normalisation — raw dot product not reliable as similarity
vecs_raw = model.encode(texts)                      # ‖v‖ varies

# With normalisation — dot product == cosine
vecs_norm = model.encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)  # ‖v‖ = 1

# These are now identical
dot_sim = vecs_norm @ vecs_norm.T                 # (3, 3)

print(dot_sim.round(3))
# [[1.    0.784 0.236]
#  [0.784 1.    0.227]
#  [0.236 0.227 1.   ]]
Dot Product in FAISS
FAISS uses dot product as the inner distance metric for
IndexFlatIP
(IP = inner product). Passing normalised vectors makes it a cosine index.

import faiss, numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer

model = SentenceTransformer("BAAI/bge-m3")

docs = ["cats are small carnivores",
        "dogs are loyal companions",
        "Python is a programming language",
        "machine learning models learn from data"]

# Normalise at index time
vecs = model.encode(docs, normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)

index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(vecs.shape[1])  # inner product index
index.add(vecs)

# Query — also normalise the query vector
query = model.encode(["feline pets"], normalize_embeddings=True).astype(np.float32)
scores, ids = index.search(query, k=2)

for r in range(2):
    print(f"{scores[0, r]:.3f}  {docs[ids[0, r]]}")
# 0.812  cats are small carnivores
# 0.753  dogs are loyal companions
When to Use Raw Dot Product (Not Cosine)

Some recommendation and two-tower models deliberately train user and item embeddings with varying magnitudes. The magnitude encodes confidence or popularity — a high-magnitude item vector means the model is confident this item is popular and relevant. In these systems, raw dot product (without normalising) is the correct similarity measure.

# Two-tower model where magnitude is meaningful
# user_vecs and item_vecs are NOT normalised
user_vecs = np.random.randn(10, 64)   # 10 users
item_vecs = np.random.randn(100, 64)  # 100 items

# Raw dot product: accounts for both direction AND confidence
scores = user_vecs @ item_vecs.T   # (10, 100)
top_items_per_user = np.argsort(scores, axis=1)[:, -5:][:, ::-1]

# DON'T normalise here — you'd remove the magnitude signal the model was trained to use

Check the model card before choosing between cosine and raw dot product. The model's training objective determines which measure aligns with what the model learned to produce. Mismatching produces silently wrong rankings.
Speed Comparison

Dot product is marginally faster than cosine because cosine requires two norm computations in addition to the dot product. With pre-normalised vectors, cosine reduces to dot product so there is no speed difference. The real win from normalisation is at index build time, not query time.

import numpy as np, time

N, D = 100_000, 1024
vecs = np.random.randn(N, D).astype(np.float32)
query = np.random.randn(1, D).astype(np.float32)

# Cosine (includes 2 norm computations per pair)
t0 = time.perf_counter()
norms = np.linalg.norm(vecs, axis=1)
scores_cos = (vecs @ query.T).flatten() / (norms * np.linalg.norm(query))
print(f"cosine: {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.1f} ms")

# Pre-normalise once
vecs_norm = vecs / norms[:, None]
query_norm = query / np.linalg.norm(query)

# Dot product (cosine-equivalent after normalisation)
t0 = time.perf_counter()
scores_dot = (vecs_norm @ query_norm.T).flatten()
print(f"dot (pre-normed): {(time.perf_counter()-t0)*1000:.1f} ms")
# dot (pre-normed): ~30% faster because norms already computed
What It Is
The dot product (also called inner product or scalar product) is the sum of element-wise products of two vectors. For vectors
a
and
b
of dimension n, it produces a single scalar value:
a·b = Σ a_i * b_i

. In the context of embedding search, the dot product between a query vector and a document vector is the most computationally efficient similarity measure available — it reduces to a single call to a BLAS
dgemv
routine or, at scale, to a dense matrix multiplication.
The dot product has a geometric interpretation: it equals the product of the magnitudes of the two vectors multiplied by the cosine of the angle between them —
a·b = ‖a‖ ‖b‖ cos(θ)

. This means the dot product conflates two things: directional similarity (captured by the cosine term) and magnitude. When you want pure directional similarity independent of vector length — the typical requirement for semantic text search — you normalise both vectors to unit length first. After normalisation,
, so the dot product equals cos(θ) exactly.

Understanding this relationship is essential for building correct embedding search systems. Most modern sentence embedding models (BGE, E5, all-MiniLM, Nomic Embed) are trained with a contrastive objective that encourages semantically similar texts to have similar unit vectors. Their model cards explicitly state 'use cosine similarity' — meaning normalise and then dot-product. A smaller class of models — especially two-tower recommendation models — are trained to encode popularity or confidence in the vector magnitude. For these, raw dot product (without normalisation) is the correct metric.
Why It Matters

In a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) system, the similarity metric is in the critical path of every query. At 10,000 documents and 768-dimensional embeddings, computing cosine similarity naively requires computing 10,000 dot products plus 10,000 norm computations plus 10,000 divisions. If vectors are pre-normalised at index time, the norms are already 1.0 and you only need 10,000 dot products — the divisions are free. This is a meaningful throughput difference at scale, and it explains why every production vector database (FAISS, Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant) pre-normalises vectors for cosine indexes.

The dot product also determines what vector index structures are applicable. FAISS's most powerful indexes (IVF, HNSW, PQ) work on inner product directly. Approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) algorithms can exploit the structure of the dot product to build hierarchical indexes that retrieve approximate top-k results in O(log n) rather than O(n). The entire infrastructure of production-scale semantic search is built on efficient dot product computation.

Choosing the wrong similarity metric is a silent failure. If you use raw dot product with non-normalised vectors, high-frequency documents (which tend to have larger embedding norms due to more training signal) will systematically outscore semantically relevant but lower-norm documents. Conversely, if you normalise vectors for a two-tower recommendation model that encoded popularity in magnitude, you lose the confidence signal and recommendation quality degrades. The choice of metric must match the model's training objective.
Architecture — Dot Product in Vector Search

attrs.alt: Dot product vector search pipeline: query encoding, normalisation, FAISS IndexFlatIP computation, and when not to normalise

content.text: Benefits
Maximum computational efficiency.
A single BLAS dgemv call. Optimised by decades of hardware and library engineering. On modern CPUs with AVX-512, a 768-dim dot product completes in nanoseconds.
Equivalent to cosine after normalisation.
Pre-normalise once at index time; all subsequent query-time computations are pure dot products with no division overhead.
GPU-parallelisable.
Matrix-vector multiplication (many dot products in parallel) maps perfectly to GPU SIMD architecture. FAISS on GPU computes millions of dot products per second.
Compatible with ANN indexes.
HNSW, IVF, and PQ indexes in FAISS and other libraries are designed around inner product. They enable sub-linear retrieval time on billion-scale indexes.
Encodes magnitude when needed.
For recommendation and ranking models where the model embeds confidence in vector length, raw dot product is the correct similarity measure that utilises the full information in the embedding.
Trade-offs
Magnitude sensitivity without normalisation.

Vectors with larger L2 norms systematically score higher in raw dot product, regardless of directional similarity. This biases results toward long documents or high-frequency entities if using non-normalised embeddings.
Not bounded.

Unlike cosine similarity (bounded [-1, 1]) or Euclidean distance (bounded [0, ∞]), raw dot product is unbounded. This can complicate threshold-based filtering and makes cross-collection comparison unreliable.
Normalisation cost is real (once).
You must normalise every document at index time and every query at query time. For a billion-document index, batch normalisation is an engineering step that must be planned.
Model-metric coupling.

The correct metric is model-specific and is not visible in the embedding vector itself. Switching embedding models without checking the model card's recommended metric is a common source of silent quality regression.
When to Use It
Use dot product (after normalisation) when:
Building semantic text search or RAG retrieval with any standard sentence embedding model (BGE, E5, all-MiniLM, Nomic Embed, Cohere Embed). These models are all trained for cosine similarity.
Querying a FAISS IndexFlatIP — pre-normalise all vectors and the IP index becomes a cosine index.
You need maximum query throughput and have already paid the normalisation cost at index time.
Use raw dot product (without normalisation) when:
The model card explicitly states 'use dot product' (e.g., some OpenAI text-embedding-ada models, two-tower recommendation models trained with in-batch negatives where magnitude encodes confidence).
Building recommendation systems where item popularity or salience is encoded in embedding magnitude.
Real-World Example — Production RAG with Caching

A documentation search system indexes 100,000 technical documents using BGE-large-en-v1.5 (1024-dim embeddings). It pre-normalises all document vectors at index time and stores them in a FAISS IndexFlatIP. At query time, only a single normalisation (the query vector) is needed before issuing the dot product search.

# Production RAG retrieval with pre-normalised dot product search
# pip install faiss-cpu sentence-transformers numpy
import faiss
import numpy as np
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from pathlib import Path
import pickle


class DocSearchIndex:
    """
    Production-style vector index using FAISS IndexFlatIP with pre-normalised vectors.
    Dot product on normalised vectors == cosine similarity, with no per-query norm cost.
    """

    def __init__(self, model_name: str = "BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5"):
        self.encoder = SentenceTransformer(model_name)
        self.dim = self.encoder.get_sentence_embedding_dimension()
        self.index = faiss.IndexFlatIP(self.dim)  # Inner Product index
        self.id_to_doc: dict[int, str] = {}

    def add_documents(self, documents: list[str], batch_size: int = 256) -> None:
        """Encode, normalise, and add documents to the index."""
        print(f"Indexing {len(documents)} documents...")
        all_vecs = []
        for i in range(0, len(documents), batch_size):
            batch = documents[i : i + batch_size]
            # normalize_embeddings=True ensures ‖v‖=1 — critical for IP == cosine
            vecs = self.encoder.encode(
                batch,
                normalize_embeddings=True,
                show_progress_bar=False,
            ).astype(np.float32)
            all_vecs.append(vecs)
            for j, doc in enumerate(batch):
                self.id_to_doc[i + j] = doc

        all_vecs = np.vstack(all_vecs)
        self.index.add(all_vecs)
        print(f"Index has {self.index.ntotal} vectors of dim {self.dim}")

    def search(self, query: str, k: int = 5) -> list[tuple[float, str]]:
        """Encode query, normalise, and search. Returns (score, doc) pairs."""
        # query also normalised — dot product == cosine, score in [-1, 1]
        q_vec = self.encoder.encode(
            [query], normalize_embeddings=True
        ).astype(np.float32)

        scores, ids = self.index.search(q_vec, k)
        results = []
        for score, idx in zip(scores[0], ids[0]):
            if idx != -1:  # -1 means no result found
                results.append((float(score), self.id_to_doc[idx]))
        return results

    def save(self, path: str) -> None:
        """Persist the index and document map."""
        faiss.write_index(self.index, f"{path}.faiss")
        with open(f"{path}.docs.pkl", "wb") as f:
            pickle.dump(self.id_to_doc, f)

    @classmethod
    def load(cls, path: str, model_name: str = "BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5") -> "DocSearchIndex":
        inst = cls(model_name)
        inst.index = faiss.read_index(f"{path}.faiss")
        with open(f"{path}.docs.pkl", "rb") as f:
            inst.id_to_doc = pickle.load(f)
        return inst


# Example usage:
DOCUMENTS = [
    "The Python GIL prevents true parallelism in CPU-bound threads.",
    "FastAPI supports async request handlers and dependency injection.",
    "FAISS IndexIVFPQ enables approximate nearest neighbor search on billion-scale datasets.",
    "Transformers use self-attention to compute contextual embeddings.",
    "Postgres JSONB columns support GIN indexes for fast JSON querying.",
]

# idx = DocSearchIndex()
# idx.add_documents(DOCUMENTS)
# results = idx.search("how does attention work in neural nets?", k=2)
# for score, doc in results:
#     print(f"{score:.3f}  {doc}")
# 0.742  Transformers use self-attention to compute contextual embeddings.
# 0.521  FAISS IndexIVFPQ enables approximate nearest neighbor search...
Common Mistakes
Not checking the model card for the recommended metric.

Using raw dot product with a cosine-trained model, or cosine with a dot-product-trained model, produces silently wrong rankings. Always look up the embedding model's recommended similarity metric before building the index.
Normalising query but not documents (or vice versa).
The dot-product-as-cosine equivalence requires both vectors to be normalised. Normalising only the query and leaving document vectors un-normalised produces incorrect scores. Normalise both.
Using IndexFlatL2 instead of IndexFlatIP for cosine search.
If both vectors are unit-normalised, L2 distance and cosine are related by
L2² = 2(1 - cos)
, so they give the same ranking. However, IndexFlatIP is marginally faster and its scores are directly interpretable as cosine values. Prefer IndexFlatIP for cosine search.
Re-normalising document vectors at query time.
Normalisation should happen once at index build time. Normalising again at query time is wasted work — the vectors are already unit-length. Only the query vector needs normalising at query time.
Interpreting absolute dot product values across different models.

A score of 0.8 from model A and 0.8 from model B do not represent the same degree of similarity unless both models were trained to the same objective. Dot product scores are only comparable within a single model.
Best Practices
Always pass normalize_embeddings=True when indexing.
In SentenceTransformers, Hugging Face Inference, and most SDKs, there is an explicit normalisation flag. Enable it at index time. This enables pure dot product (no division) at query time.
Use FAISS IndexFlatIP as your baseline.

For datasets up to ~100k vectors, IndexFlatIP is exact, fast, and requires no hyperparameter tuning. Only move to approximate indexes (IVF, HNSW) when IndexFlatIP query latency exceeds your SLA at production query volume.
Store normalised vectors in float32, not float64.
float32 is 4 bytes per dimension vs 8 bytes for float64. For 100k vectors at 768 dimensions, this is 300 MB vs 600 MB. Embedding models do not produce meaningful precision beyond float32.
For approximate search, move to IndexIVFFlat or HNSWFlat.
These indexes support inner product and achieve O(log n) retrieval. Configure nlist (number of IVF clusters) to approximately sqrt(n) for good recall/speed tradeoff.
Performance and Scalability

# Benchmarking: dot product vs cosine vs Euclidean at scale
import numpy as np
import time

N = 100_000   # number of index vectors
D = 768       # embedding dimension (typical for BERT-base class models)

# Simulate a pre-normalised index and query vector
raw = np.random.randn(N, D).astype(np.float32)
norms = np.linalg.norm(raw, axis=1, keepdims=True)
vecs_norm = raw / norms           # pre-normalised (done once at index time)
query_norm = np.random.randn(1, D).astype(np.float32)
query_norm /= np.linalg.norm(query_norm)

# --- Method 1: cosine (computes norms every time) ---
t0 = time.perf_counter()
vec_norms = np.linalg.norm(raw, axis=1)  # re-computing norms
scores_cos = (raw @ query_norm.T).flatten() / (vec_norms * float(np.linalg.norm(query_norm)))
elapsed_cos = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
print(f"Cosine (naive):        {elapsed_cos:.1f} ms")

# --- Method 2: dot product on pre-normalised vectors (== cosine) ---
t0 = time.perf_counter()
scores_dot = (vecs_norm @ query_norm.T).flatten()
elapsed_dot = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
print(f"Dot product (normed):  {elapsed_dot:.1f} ms  ({elapsed_cos/elapsed_dot:.1f}x faster)")

# --- Method 3: Euclidean distance (most expensive) ---
t0 = time.perf_counter()
diff = raw - query_norm  # broadcasting
scores_l2 = np.linalg.norm(diff, axis=1)
elapsed_l2 = (time.perf_counter() - t0) * 1000
print(f"Euclidean:             {elapsed_l2:.1f} ms")

# Verify rankings match between cosine (naive) and dot (pre-normed)
assert np.allclose(scores_cos, scores_dot, atol=1e-5), "Rankings should match!"
print("Rankings verified: dot product on normed vecs == cosine similarity")

# Expected output (varies by hardware):
# Cosine (naive):        42.3 ms
# Dot product (normed):  11.8 ms  (3.6x faster)
# Euclidean:             95.1 ms
# Rankings verified: dot product on normed vecs == cosine similarity
Flat search scales linearly:
O(N * D) per query. At 1M vectors and 768 dimensions, FAISS IndexFlatIP on a single CPU core takes ~500ms. Use GPU (FAISS-GPU) for <50ms at this scale.
ANN indexes reduce to O(D * log N):
HNSW with M=32, ef=50 gives ~95% recall at 10x throughput compared to flat search. The trade-off is 2–3× higher RAM usage for the graph structure.
Quantisation (PQ):
Product quantisation compresses 768-dim float32 vectors to 96 bytes (8x compression), enabling billion-scale indexes in <100 GB RAM. Recall drops to ~80–90% at high compression ratios.
Tooling and Ecosystem
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search).
The industry standard for dense vector search. IndexFlatIP, IndexIVFFlat, IndexHNSWFlat all support inner product. Available on CPU and GPU.
pip install faiss-cpu
NumPy.
For batch dot products:
vecs @ query.T
is the fastest Python-level dot product call for arrays up to ~10M vectors.
SentenceTransformers.
Provides
encode(texts, normalize_embeddings=True)
for direct normalised output. Also provides
util.dot_score()
and
util.cos_sim()
convenience functions.
Pinecone / Qdrant / Weaviate.

Managed vector databases that use dot product internally. Pinecone's 'cosine' metric pre-normalises at upsert time; 'dotproduct' metric uses raw dot product. Choose correctly based on your embedding model.
Security and Reliability
Embedding inversion attacks.

Dot product scores reveal similarity structure. In some scenarios, repeatedly querying with probing vectors can reconstruct private document content from a RAG index. Limit the number of returned results per query and add result-count rate limiting.
Numerical stability.

Dot products of high-dimensional float32 vectors can accumulate floating-point error. For D > 1000, verify that dot product scores between identical vectors are within 1e-5 of 1.0. Use float32 (not float16) for index storage to maintain precision.
Poisoning attacks.

An attacker who can add documents to the index can craft embeddings that score highly for many queries (adversarial embedding injection). Rate-limit index writes and monitor the distribution of retrieval scores for anomalies.
Key Takeaways
The dot product a·b = Σ a_i * b_i is the fastest similarity computation available, reducing to a single BLAS matrix multiply call.
When both vectors are unit-normalised (‖a‖ = ‖b‖ = 1), dot product equals cosine similarity exactly. Pre-normalise at index time and use pure dot product at query time — zero division cost.
Use FAISS IndexFlatIP for cosine search on pre-normalised vectors. It is exact, requires no hyperparameter tuning, and is 3–5× faster than computing cosine with per-query norm recomputation.

For two-tower recommendation models that encode confidence in vector magnitude, use raw dot product without normalisation. Check the model card — using the wrong metric produces silently wrong rankings.
Always normalise both query and document vectors, not just one. Partial normalisation produces incorrect scores.
Flat dot product search is O(N * D) per query. For datasets beyond ~100k vectors, use approximate indexes (HNSW, IVF) that achieve O(D log N) at ~95% recall.

Dot product scores are not comparable across different embedding models. A score of 0.8 means different things for different models. Evaluate similarity thresholds empirically on your specific model and domain.
Summary
Situation
Use
Vectors pre-normalised to ‖v‖ = 1
Dot product (== cosine, faster)
Vectors NOT normalised, magnitude irrelevant
Cosine similarity (normalise on-the-fly)
Two-tower model trained with magnitude signal
Raw dot product (do NOT normalise)
FAISS index type for cosine search
IndexFlatIP
with normalised vectors
